value: The microstructure evolution and mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel at different hot-rolling reductions are investigated, and the strengthening and strain-hardening mechanisms are analyzed.
Enhanced mechanical properties of a Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel by increasing hot-rolling reduction
Actual composition (wt%): Mn 30.9, Al 8.7, C 0.9, Nb 0.45, Mo 0.45, W 0.43, Fe balance. Density measured: 6.82 g/cm³, ~13% density reduction compared to pure iron.
Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 45% (thickness 60 mm) in 4 passes, followed by water quenching.
Forged from ingot at 1150°C to 110 mm billet and air cooled prior to reheating.
Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 68% (thickness 35 mm) in 6 passes, followed by water quenching.
Hot-forged billet reheated at 1150°C, hot-rolled to a reduction of 82% (thickness 20 mm) in 9 passes, followed by water quenching.
Mean grain size excluding twins: 45.11 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 21.82 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 67%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior
Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
Mean grain size excluding twins: 27.02 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 11.55 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 60%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior
Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
Mean grain size excluding twins: 18.80 μm; effective grain size including twin boundaries: 7.02 μm; Σ3 twin boundary fraction: 52%
Submicron particles homogeneously distributed; micron-sized particles along rolling direction at grain boundaries and interior
Equiaxed fully austenitic microstructure with annealing twins, containing nano-sized κ-carbides and (Nb,Mo,W)C particles.
Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.
With increasing hot-rolling reduction, grain size refined due to enhanced DRX and PSN, dislocation density increased, while precipitate types and volume fractions remained similar.
Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.
Dislocations shear coherent κ-carbides, creating anti-phase boundaries and causing glide plane softening.
Dislocations bypass incoherent (Nb,Mo,W)C particles via Orowan mechanism.
Engineering stress-strain curves shown in Fig. 5a; true stress-strain and strain-hardening rate curves in Fig. 10.
Solution-treated austenitic stainless steels (SUS347J1TB, Nickel-free ASS, Super ASS) and Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys (Fe-28Mn-9Al-0.8C, Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.4-1.2C etc.)

YS of HR82 comparable to aging-hardened steels (~650 MPa) but with significantly higher ductility (50% vs. 21%) and toughness (105 J/m^2 vs. 35 J/m^2); markedly higher YS than solution-treated ASS with similar ductility and toughness.
Calculated SFE ~73 mJ/m², indicating high SFE.
Lattice constant a, Avogadro constant N_A, free energy difference ΔG^{γ→ε}, γ/ε interface energy σ

Equation (5) summarizing contributions from friction, precipitation, dislocation, and grain boundary strengthening; sub-models Eq. (6) (κ shearing), Eq. (7) ((Nb,Mo,W)C Orowan), Eq. (8) (dislocation), Eq. (9) (Hall-Petch).
Grain boundary strengthening (from HAGBs and twin boundaries) was the dominant mechanism, followed by dislocation strengthening.
σ0=203.1 MPa, Hall-Petch coefficient k_HP=733.43 MPa·μm^(1/2), Taylor factor M=3.06, shear moduli 68 GPa (κ) and 170 GPa (MC), Burgers vector b=0.26 nm, APB energy 350 mJ/m², Poisson's ratio ν.
Calculated yield strengths (Table 2) show good agreement with experimental values.
Slip band spacing refined with strain, saturating at ~50 nm for all samples; earlier saturation in finer-grained samples led to earlier microband formation and slight ductility loss.

Three-staged strain-hardening behavior: initial drop (stage I) due to glide plane softening, increase (stage II) from slip band refinement and long-range stress, slow decline (stage III) from microband formation.

Fractography: all samples showed ductile fracture with dimples and microvoids; intergranular (Nb,Mo,W)C particles act as microcrack nucleation sites, but effect mitigated by debonding of intragranular particles.
KAM maps (Fig. 1g-i) indicate higher local strain concentrations with increasing rolling reduction, consistent with higher GND densities.
Fig. 10 shows strain-hardening rate curves; the rate maintained ~2000 MPa over a wide strain range, contributing to excellent ductility.
No image pixel data available for figures; conclusions based on text descriptions of micrographs and graphs.
